title: MCP Servers
course: Agentic Development with GitHub Copilot CLI, LakeBank, SyteLine, and MCP
prerequisites: Learner can start a safe GitHub Copilot CLI session.
Learner understands repo classification, context engineering, memory hygiene, planning, review, testing, implementation steering, LakeBank, SyteLine, and plugin repo authoring.
summary: This module teaches how Model Context Protocol, or MCP, servers fit into your team's agentic development workflow.
Your team has three MCP servers:
1. SQL Server MCP
2. Power-Flex MCP
3. VersionOneMCP

This module should not be finalized until the instructor documents what each server can do, which actions are read-only, which actions can write, which environments they can access, and which use cases are safe for training.
The goal of this module is not to teach developers to use MCP casually.
The goal is to teach developers when MCP is useful, when it is required, when it is prohibited, and how to turn MCP output into reviewable evidence.
MCP extends Copilot's ability to access external systems. That makes it powerful and risky.
Developers must learn to:
- identify whether MCP is available
- understand MCP server capabilities
- respect read-only/write boundaries
- protect customer and production data
- avoid destructive actions
- cite MCP evidence in markdown artifacts
- avoid leaking sensitive data into PRs or shared memory
- use MCP only after repo classification, context engineering, research, and planning make it appropriate
- stop and ask for human review when MCP permissions or environment scope are unclear

This module defaults to read-only discovery, intake, and safe-use planning. No write-capable MCP action should be used in training unless explicitly approved by the instructor and documented in this module.
whyItMatters: GitHub Copilot CLI supports adding MCP servers. GitHub's documentation notes that the GitHub MCP server is built into Copilot CLI and that additional MCP servers can be added. It also notes that an enterprise registry URL and allowlist policy may control which servers can run.

The Model Context Protocol specification describes servers as exposing features such as resources, prompts, and tools. Resources provide context and data; prompts provide templated messages and workflows; tools are functions the model can execute. The specification also warns that MCP enables powerful capabilities through arbitrary data access and code execution paths.

MCP security guidance emphasizes authorization, least privilege, consent, and protection from tool misuse. OWASP's MCP security guidance describes MCP as a standard interface layer for AI applications to connect to external tools, data sources, and services, while also requiring security controls around trust, tools, and data exposure.
For your team, MCP can make agentic development much stronger:
- SQL Server MCP can help investigate schema, stored procedures, data patterns, and read-only diagnostics.
- Power-Flex MCP can help validate product concepts, configuration-driven behavior, business rules, and workflow alignment.
- VersionOneMCP can help tie work to stories, acceptance criteria, gaps, and release context.
But MCP can also create serious risk:
- copying sensitive results into markdown or PRs
- letting Copilot invoke tools without understanding impact
- treating MCP output as verified truth without review
- mixing external data into durable memory
- bypassing normal support, QA, or product workflows
This module teaches controlled MCP use.
howItFits: Earlier modules taught the core workflow and plugin fundamentals.
Module 13 teaches MCP use as an evidence-gathering and validation capability.
MCP should usually enter the workflow at specific points:
- gather evidence from SQL, Power-Flex, or VersionOne
- confirm acceptance criteria, data/config dependencies, or product rules
- identify missing evidence or external-system risk
- create read-only validation checks or manual validation support
- generally avoid MCP writes; use MCP only for approved read-only validation unless explicitly authorized
- summarize MCP-derived evidence safely without exposing sensitive data
MCP should not be the first step. Repo classification and context engineering still come first.
learningObjectives: Explain what MCP is in the context of GitHub Copilot CLI.
Explain why MCP increases both capability and risk.
Identify whether MCP servers are configured or available.
Explain the difference between MCP resources, prompts, and tools.
Explain why read-only and write-capable actions must be separated.
Explain when SQL Server MCP should be used.
Explain when Power-Flex MCP should be used.
Explain when VersionOneMCP should be used.
Record MCP evidence safely in markdown.
Identify data that must not be copied into docs, memory, PRs, or plugin repos.
Decide when MCP use requires human approval.
Decide when MCP should block planning or implementation until evidence is gathered.
Explain why MCP output still requires human verification.
keyConceptsMarkdown: ### Concept 1: MCP connects Copilot to external capabilities
MCP is a protocol that allows AI applications to connect to external tools, data sources, and workflows.
In practical terms, MCP can let Copilot ask questions or perform actions against systems outside the local repo.
This makes MCP valuable for SDLC tasks that cannot be answered from source code alone.
### Concept 2: MCP tools are not just context
A file reference gives Copilot context.
An MCP tool may let Copilot execute an action.
Developers must understand the difference before using MCP.
A safe training course should start with read-only MCP use only.
### Concept 3: Read-only does not mean risk-free
A read-only MCP query can still expose sensitive data.
They should not paste raw sensitive data into:
Use sanitized summaries and cite internal evidence carefully.
### Concept 4: MCP evidence should be captured as evidence, not memory
MCP findings are usually task evidence.
Do not put raw MCP output into durable memory.
A durable memory candidate may be created only if the finding is:
- not customer-specific unless approved for that repo
### Concept 5: SQL Server MCP should be guarded carefully
SQL Server MCP may be useful for:
- data validation in a sandbox/dev environment
SQL Server MCP should be prohibited or tightly controlled for:
- raw data dumps into docs or PRs
Training should default SQL Server MCP to read-only, dev/test environment only.
### Concept 6: Power-Flex MCP should explain product behavior safely
Power-Flex MCP may be useful for:
- checking whether a proposed change aligns with product rules
Power-Flex MCP should be prohibited or tightly controlled for:
- exposing sensitive customer or product data
- replacing product owner or senior developer judgment
Training should use Power-Flex MCP to support research and review, not to bypass review.
- linking PR summaries to work items
- generating test ideas from acceptance criteria
VersionOneMCP should be prohibited or tightly controlled for:
- exposing private planning details in public docs
- treating a story as complete when criteria are vague
Training should use VersionOneMCP as a read-only requirements source unless your team explicitly approves write actions.
### Concept 8: MCP use should be planned
Do not ask Copilot to "use MCP" generically.
A good MCP prompt should specify:
Use SQL Server MCP only for read-only schema discovery in the development environment.
Summarize table/procedure relationships relevant to the research question in docs/mcp/<work-item>-mcp-evidence.md.
### Concept 9: MCP can block planning or implementation
If a plan depends on unknown external data, do not guess.
- expected behavior depends on a VersionOne acceptance criterion
- implementation depends on SQL schema details
- support incident depends on read-only data diagnosis
- SyteLine work depends on database object behavior
In those cases, the correct next step may be MCP evidence collection before planning or implementation.
### Concept 10: MCP availability can vary by developer or repo
Record availability and limitations in the MCP intake artifact.
| Schema or stored procedure discovery | SQL Server MCP | Identify tables/procs related to labor commit. |
| Read-only diagnostic query | SQL Server MCP | Confirm whether test data matches expected state in dev. |
| Product configuration behavior | Power-Flex MCP | Check whether a workflow is configuration-driven. |
| Product rule validation | Power-Flex MCP | Confirm whether plan aligns with Power-Flex rules. |
| Acceptance criteria | VersionOneMCP | Extract story criteria and identify gaps. |
| Requirement ambiguity | VersionOneMCP | Identify missing story details before planning. |
| PR summary alignment | VersionOneMCP | Compare implementation evidence to story. |
| Customer-sensitive investigation | Usually human-reviewed first | Decide whether MCP use is safe and what may be summarized. |
| Production data change | Prohibited in training | Escalate to approved support/database process. |
instructorDemoMarkdown: Create an MCP safe-use plan before using MCP
Show developers that MCP should be planned and governed before it is used.
## Demo step 1: show a weak MCP prompt
Use MCP to figure this out.
## Demo step 2: collect MCP server intake
Use the required instructor intake prompts.
This should be done before hands-on developer labs.
## Demo step 3: run the MCP intake prompt
Run this in GitHub Copilot CLI:
We are preparing the MCP Servers module for an internal agentic development course.
- Do not call MCP tools yet.
- data that must not be copied
Mark unknown values as TODO for instructor input.
Do not call MCP tools yet.
## Demo step 4: review the safe-use plan
- Are production and customer-data risks addressed?
- Is human review required where appropriate?

lab.title: Create an MCP server intake and safe-use plan
beforeYouStart: I opened a terminal in the training repo or appropriate sandbox repo.
I checked the working tree status.
I cleared stale context or started a fresh session.
I understand that default lab does not call MCP tools.
I will not change MCP configuration.
I will not query customer data.
I will not run write-capable MCP actions.
I will not copy sensitive output into memory-bank, PRs, memory, or plugin repos.

prompts.label: Lab prompt
promptText: We are practicing MCP server governance for an internal agentic development course.
- Do not call MCP tools yet.
- Do not run write-capable MCP actions.
- Do not install or modify plugins.
Include status, date, instructor, reviewer, environment, and approved-for-training status.
### Data That Must Not Be Copied
Then create docs/mcp/mcp-safe-use-plan.md with these sections:
Include status, repo, branch, work item if any, and approved MCP servers.
Explain when source code, repo docs, LakeBank, SyteLine, or VersionOne story text may not be enough.
List SQL Server MCP, Power-Flex MCP, VersionOneMCP, or none.
List exact approved actions. Use TODO where instructor input is needed.
Include production writes, destructive actions, raw customer data extraction, secrets exposure, uncontrolled SQL generation, MCP config changes, and memory updates from raw MCP data.
Explain what may be summarized and what must not be copied.
List developer, senior developer, DBA, product owner, support, QA, or security review needs.
Create safe read-only prompt templates for:
- Do not assume production access is allowed.
- Use TODO where details are missing.

prompts.label: Optional instructor-approved MCP evidence prompt
promptText: We are using MCP for instructor-approved read-only evidence collection.
- Use only the approved MCP server and action.
- Do not access production unless explicitly approved.
- Do not return raw customer-sensitive data.

verificationChecklist: Did Copilot avoid calling MCP tools in the default lab?
Did Copilot avoid changing MCP configuration?
Did Copilot avoid customer data queries?
Did Copilot avoid write-capable MCP actions?
Did Copilot keep this as intake/safe-use documentation only, with no source changes or durable memory updates?
Did Copilot avoid inventing tool names?
Did Copilot mark unknowns as TODO?
Did Copilot separate read-only and write-capable actions?
Did Copilot include data handling rules?
Did Copilot include human review requirements?
Did Copilot create safe prompt templates?
Did Copilot label assumptions and unknowns?

evidence.summaryPrompt: Write 3-5 sentences describing what MCP servers are available or unknown, what boundaries exist, and what must be clarified before live MCP use.
reflectionQuestions: What MCP servers does the team have?
Which details are still TODO for SQL Server MCP?
Which details are still TODO for Power-Flex MCP?
Which details are still TODO for VersionOneMCP?
For each server (SQL Server MCP, Power-Flex MCP, VersionOneMCP), what actions are approved read-only?
What actions are write-capable or prohibited?
What environments can the MCP servers access?
What data must not be copied into docs, PRs, memory, or plugin repos?
When should MCP use stop for human review?
Which prompt template explicitly names server, allowed action, environment, and stop/escalation rule?

skillCheck.question: Why must this module define per-server actions, environments, and prohibited use before live MCP calls?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Because MCP safety is server-specific. Before any live use, the team must document each server's actions, read/write boundaries, environments, data handling limits, and stop conditions so prompts are constrained and reviewable.

skillCheck.question: What is one concrete risk for each server and its control?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: SQL Server MCP: sensitive data exposure; control with approved read-only queries and strict data-handling rules. Power-Flex MCP: unintended operational impact; control with allowed-action boundaries and human review. VersionOneMCP: requirement/traceability drift from incorrect edits; control with read-only story retrieval unless explicit approval exists.

skillCheck.question: Why is read-only MCP not risk-free?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Read-only queries can still expose sensitive information such as customer data, production identifiers, financial data, configuration, support details, or private requirements. The data must be summarized safely and not copied raw into docs, PRs, memory, or plugin repos.

skillCheck.question: When should SQL Server MCP be used?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: SQL Server MCP may be used for approved read-only schema discovery, stored procedure discovery, diagnostics, data/config dependency research, and validation in approved environments. It should not be used for production writes, destructive commands, data fixes, or raw data dumps in training.

skillCheck.question: When should Power-Flex MCP be used?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Power-Flex MCP may be used to validate product concepts, configuration-driven behavior, business rules, workflow alignment, product metadata, or support troubleshooting, within approved read-only boundaries.

skillCheck.question: When should VersionOneMCP be used?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: VersionOneMCP may be used to read stories, acceptance criteria, related work, release context, requirement gaps, and test ideas. It should not edit work items or change status unless explicitly approved.

skillCheck.question: What should happen before live MCP use in this course?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: The instructor must fill out the MCP server intake, including tool/action names, read-only and write-capable actions, environments, data risks, safe and prohibited use cases, example prompts, output format, logging, approvals, and data handling rules.

summaryMarkdown: In this module, you learned how MCP servers fit into the agentic development workflow.
- MCP connects Copilot to external tools, resources, and prompts
- SQL Server MCP, Power-Flex MCP, and VersionOneMCP need documented capabilities and boundaries
- MCP evidence should be captured safely
- raw sensitive data should not be copied into docs, PRs, memory, or plugin repos
- MCP output still requires human verification
- MCP can block planning or implementation when external evidence is required
- live MCP use should not happen until the intake and safe-use plan are approved
This module intentionally prompts the instructor for details before concrete MCP demos and labs are finalized.
readinessChecklist: I can explain what MCP is.
I can explain why MCP increases both capability and risk.
I can explain the difference between MCP resources, prompts, and tools.
I can explain why read-only MCP is not risk-free.
I can identify when SQL Server MCP may be useful.
I can identify when Power-Flex MCP may be useful.
I can identify when VersionOneMCP may be useful.
I can explain data handling rules for MCP evidence.
I can identify when MCP use requires human review.
I can explain why live MCP demos need instructor-approved server details.
commonMistakes: Asking Copilot to "use MCP" without naming server, action, or environment.
Assuming an MCP server is read-only.
Copying raw customer data into markdown.
Running write-capable MCP actions without approval.
Treating MCP output as verified truth.
Putting raw MCP output into memory bank.
Putting customer-specific MCP findings into plugin repos.
Skipping human review when MCP evidence affects plan or implementation.
Forgetting that MCP access may vary by developer, repo, environment, or enterprise policy.

Module 12: SyteLine Plugin Value

Next you will focus on SyteLine plugin value and how it supports development decisions in existing repos.
